| 32 | 2026-07-08 | delivery | anyllm | anyllm-v0.2.0 | shelf |  | Evolved anyllm from the v0.1 sync `complete(prompt)->str` to an async, structured superset returning a rich Completion (token/cost/latency) with fail-loud AnyLLMError. a2web's rich Provider tier was PROMOTED IN, not split as a sibling, per resolution 0007 (the monotonicity test: the merged contract only expands exposure — rich return, loud failure — so converge). Four backends: claude-code-cli (stdlib), claude-code-sdk, anthropic-api, openai-compatible (each SDK an opt-in extra). anyllm-v0.1.0 stays tagged; a2kay remains on it and reconciles to v0.2.0 when it next touches its LLM seam. Reconcile-later notes surfaced by the a2web adoption (green on branch work/adopt-anyllm-v0.2.0, verdict pending merge): (1) adapter .name strings (anthropic-api/claude-code-sdk/openai-compatible) now differ from a2web's registry ids (anthropic/claude-code/openai_compatible) by design; (2) the [openai] extra pins openai <2, a version ceiling the shelf now imposes on consumers; (3) LLMProvider requires a new available() member and adapters no longer raise on construction (the surface-state split) — duck-typed consumer mocks must add available(); (4) the cache-tier token->cost accounting + PromptParts ride inside anyllm — extract as a shared T0 only if a non-LLM holder ever wants them. |
| 33 | 2026-07-09 | adopted | anyllm | anyllm-v0.2.0 | a2web | adopted | a2web deleted its local llm_extract/providers and pinned anyllm-v0.2.0. Green: 986 passed, 89.81% coverage. Extractor/Judge catch AnyLLMError at their own seam to degrade to an empty Completion, keeping anyllm's fail-loud contract intact while a2web's degrade-to-raw path fires unchanged. Follow-up landed on the shelf side: the openai extra's <2 ceiling was lifted (openai>=1.50, verified against 2.44.0, 36/36 anyllm tests green) since it was a defensive pin with no code dependency on the 1.x line. |
| 34 | 2026-07-09 | delivery | convert-md | convert-md-v0.4.0 | shelf |  | EVOLVE: swapped the `.pdf` chain's primary engine from docling to pymupdf4llm (docling kept as fallback, not dropped). Forced by the pdf-engine-verification change (openspec/changes/2026-07-09-pdf-engine-verification): a live-run comparison harness (docling, pymupdf4llm, markitdown[pdf], unstructured[pdf], each isolated/transient per D4 except docling+markitdown which are real [documents] deps) against a 4-category public-domain-ish corpus found docling and pymupdf4llm tied on table structure, heading hierarchy, and reading order (bench/results/2026-07-09-findings.md) -- markitdown and unstructured both scored meaningfully worse (scrambled reading order, false-positive/missing headings, fabricated or absent tables). Since there was no clear docling fidelity margin, D3's rule (tax only earned with a margin) resolves to: no margin, no tax owed. New `convert-md[pdf]` extra ships pymupdf4llm alone (no ML/GPU, no docling, no typer conflict) -- the conflict-free path a2web-shaped consumers can actually install; `[documents]` keeps docling as the PDF fallback for the office-format superset. Also fixed a real platform bug hit along the way: docling's default MPS (Apple Silicon) device crashes on a float64 op in this docling/torch combo -- CPU device now forced in DoclingEngine. Additive extras, no breaking API change; a2kay on convert-md-v0.1.0/v0.3.0 unaffected until it next reconciles. |
| 35 | 2026-07-09 | delivery | convert-md | convert-md-v0.5.0 | shelf |  | EVOLVE: supersedes ledger/0034 — dropped docling entirely from the `.pdf` chain instead of keeping it as fallback. `.pdf` is now `[PymupdfLlmEngine]` only, no fallback. PRIMARY REASON (not the typer conflict): a same-day re-scoring pass judged every engine's output for LLM-semantic-recoverability (can the facts/values/relationships be recovered regardless of markdown formatting) instead of visual/structural fidelity, extending the corpus with CJK (Japanese/Chinese service manuals), math-heavy (arXiv formula-dense paper), and infographic-dense (Toyota Integrated Report financial table + timeline) files -- bench/results/2026-07-09-findings.md 'Extended corpus addendum' and design.md D5 have the full evidence. Under that lens docling has one total, unrecoverable failure mode (100% of formulas dropped as `<!-- formula-not-decoded -->` on math-heavy content, zero fallback) while pymupdf4llm's structural defects all downgrade to cosmetic (CJK glyph-ID garbage confined to decorative icon legends never real prose; a 'broken' financial table where every value still lands on its correct row/year; an oddly-stacked but complete math matrix) -- no corpus file in the full 8-file run needed docling to recover content pymupdf4llm couldn't already deliver. markitdown (the other live PDF candidate) was also re-checked under this lens and ruled out as a broader replacement: it has two of its own genuine semantic failures (table_heavy.pdf's two-column reading order gets sentences from unrelated topics fused together; math_heavy.pdf severs Archimedes' pi-bounds claim `223/71 < pi < 22/7` from its own denominators across two disconnected locations) on exactly the axes -- reading order and math -- that matter most to an LLM reader. marker remains the only engine that renders math correctly but stays excluded on unchanged cost/license grounds (GPL-3.0, >30x-worse CPU-only runtime) -- a fidelity win on one axis doesn't clear the adoption bar. SECONDARY REASON (already established in ledger/0034, restated for completeness): removing docling also removes the unconditional `typer<0.25` pin (design.md D1) from `convert-md[documents]` for every consumer, not just a2web-shaped ones -- sufficient on its own to justify demoting docling in the prior delivery, but explicitly not the deciding reason for full removal this time; recording both reasons and their relative weight so a future reader does not mistake this for a dependency-conflict-only decision. Code: `DoclingEngine` deleted from `engines.py`/`dispatch.py`/`__init__.py`; `docling` dropped from the `documents` extra and package keywords; `bench/engines/run_docling.py` kept as an isolated D4 probe (re-verifiable later, no longer a real dependency so now added to root `[tool.ty.src]` exclude alongside marker/unstructured). Minor version bump (0.4.0 -> 0.5.0) per the shelf's semver discipline for behavior-changing engine swaps. `make check` green. |
| 36 | 2026-07-09 | delivery | convert-md | convert-md-v0.6.0 | shelf |  | EVOLVE: redesigned convert_md.fidelity.grade()'s signal set after a direct calibration check found it was a rubber stamp on the same real corpus the pdf-engine-verification work already built (ledger/0034, 0035). Ran grade() against all 39 completed bench/outputs/ conversions (5 engines x 8 documents) and every single one scored 'high' -- including outputs already known to be badly broken (docling dropping 100% of formulas, markitdown fusing sentences across a column break, unstructured shattering a financial table into isolated one-value-per-line noise 450 lines apart). Root cause: none of these defects produce garbled bytes or remove all tables/headings document-wide, the only two things the original three signals (yield, whole-document structure presence, encoding-garbage density) could see -- tuning the existing thresholds could not have fixed this, the signals were structurally blind to these failure shapes. Added two new signals, both calibrated against real measured values from the same corpus, not guessed: (1) cid-glyph-leak line-fraction -- literal `(cid:N)` tokens from an unresolved PDF font ToUnicode mapping, measured at 0.075-0.126 on known-bad markitdown/unstructured CJK output vs 0.0 on clean output, threshold set at 0.03; also independently caught markitdown mangling math notation in math_heavy.pdf (cid tokens replacing absolute-value bars inside a limit expression), reinforcing rather than contradicting the earlier semantic-recoverability finding. (2) shattered-table numeric-line-fraction -- isolated short numeric-only lines, the shape a table takes when an engine drops pipe/row syntax entirely, measured at 0.562 on unstructured's known-shattered financial table vs 0.260 on the highest legitimate numeric-dense case (pymupdf4llm's jammed-but-data-intact rows) and 0.0 on real markdown tables regardless of numeric density, threshold set at 0.35. Re-ran the same calibration check after the change: 7 of 39 real conversions now correctly grade 'partial' (previously all 39 graded 'high'); zero regressions on cases already known to be fine, including the cosmetically-broken-but-semantically-intact investor_infographics tables from docling/pymupdf4llm, which the addendum had already established should NOT be downgraded. Explicitly documented as a known, accepted, unfixed limitation: semantic corruption where text stays clean/well-formed/structurally-intact but is reordered, fused, or has a claim severed from its own value elsewhere in the file (markitdown's table_heavy.pdf and math_heavy.pdf reading-order defects) remains undetectable by any model-free heuristic -- stated in fidelity.py's docstring rather than silently missed, per the same track-the-reasons discipline as ledger/0035. check_fidelity_calibration.py kept in bench/ as a reusable, re-runnable calibration tool (D4 spirit extended from engine comparison to grading-heuristic verification). Two new tests added (test_dispatch.py) calibrated to the same real thresholds. `make check` green. |
| 37 | 2026-07-09 | delivery | anyllm | anyllm-v0.3.0 | shelf |  | EVOLVE: closed docs/backlog.md's 'anyllm.ProviderName enum' Build item, standalone rather than deferred to a2web's dependency-upgrade sweep (the item's stated trigger) -- the shelf-side half was self-contained and didn't need to wait. Added anyllm.base.ProviderName, a StrEnum with the four existing adapter-name values (claude-code-cli, claude-code-sdk, anthropic-api, openai-compatible). Typed LLMProvider.name and every adapter's `name` class attribute against it; select.py's DEFAULT_PROVIDER/_KNOWN/build_adapter's branch dispatch now compare against enum members instead of bare-string literals. Purely additive: a StrEnum member equals its string value, so every existing caller comparing against or passing the literal string (build_adapter('anthropic-api'), adapter.name == 'anthropic-api') keeps working unchanged -- verified via the pre-existing test_adapters.py assertions, no test edits needed. Investigated the backlog item's other stated half -- 'a2web imports it instead of its own strings' -- against a2web's actual source (checked out locally): a2web's own vocabulary (settings.py's ProviderMode Literal and each _manifests/llm_providers/*.py's PluginManifest name) is NOT the same string set as anyllm's. anyllm has claude-code-sdk; a2web's plugin key is claude-code (no separate CLI variant registered). anyllm has anthropic-api; a2web's key is anthropic. These are a2web's own plugin-registry lookup keys (`registry["anthropic"]`), a public surface a caller can already depend on -- collapsing them onto anyllm.ProviderName's values would rename that surface, a breaking change for a2web, not a free unification. Left a2web untouched; this is now its own separate, explicit decision if/when it's wanted, not silently bundled into this delivery. `make check` green (312 passed, 88.77% cov). |
| 38 | 2026-07-09 | verification | shelf |  | shelf | pass | VERIFY: closed docs/backlog.md's 'Pre-commit-framework path' Verify item -- the `.pre-commit-hooks.yaml` `rev:` wiring (the pre-commit-framework alternative to the native-hook installer, documented in docs/consuming-the-shelf.md §2) had never been exercised by a real consumer. Built a scratch consumer repo, wired `.pre-commit-config.yaml` in the exact documented form (`repo: <shelf>`, `rev: <commit sha>`, `hooks: [id: no-local-shelf-source]`) against a real local clone of this repo at HEAD (2a1976d), ran `pre-commit install` for the real git hook (not `try-repo`, which only validates the manifest), then issued two real `git commit` invocations: one with an offending `[tool.uv.sources]` local/editable shelf override (blocked, exit 1, correct error message) and one with a clean git+tag pin (passed, exit 0, commit created). Both outcomes matched the native-hook installer's known-good behavior. No code changes needed -- the manifest and hook script were already correct; only the absence of a test was the gap. Scratch repos deleted after verification. |
| 39 | 2026-07-09 | delivery | atomic-io, duckdb-sidecar, managed-region | atomic-io-v0.1.0, duckdb-sidecar-v0.1.0, managed-region-v0.1.0 | shelf |  | PROMOTE: closed docs/backlog.md's 'a2kay's T0 primitives' Promote item, the first package to walk the full PROMOTE cycle end-to-end (worktree, extract, tag, push, repoint, delete origin copy) rather than being bootstrapped. Extracted a2kay.core.{atomic_io,duckdb_sidecar,managed_region} verbatim (self-contained, zero a2kay imports, so no adaptation needed) into three T0 packages in a worktree (../shelf-a2kay, branch work/a2kay). | 40 | 2026-07-09 | verdict | atomic-io, duckdb-sidecar, managed-region | atomic-io-v0.1.0, duckdb-sidecar-v0.1.0, managed-region-v0.1.0 | a2kay | adopted | a2kay repointed its 7 call sites (frontmatter_io, ontology_store, graph_store, audit_store, search/index, jobs/history, projection) from core/{atomic_io,duckdb_sidecar,managed_region}.py to the git-tag pins, deleted the in-repo copies + their tests, added the three packages to policies/data.json's rego upper-bound allowlist. Two test-only fixes needed to clear the shelf's stricter make check that a2kay's own weaker lint config had missed: an EM101 (raise from a variable, not a literal) and three ty not-subscriptable errors (duckdb's fetchone() returns tuple | None; bound + asserted not-None before indexing). make check green: 866 passed, 2 skipped. Pushed to a2kay main. |
| 41 | 2026-07-10 | delivery | convert-md | convert-md-v0.7.0 | shelf |  | PROMOTE/EVOLVE: made convert-md's `.docx` engine choice evidence-backed (it was inherited verbatim from R142 on one unverified claim -- pandoc's `--track-changes=all` preserves redlines -- and never benched). Extended the PDF bench harness to a docx capability-grid: 6 synthesized single-capability fixtures (design.md D2 -- synthesize for semantic formats, the inverse of the PDF corpus's real-document rule), scored pandoc (both track-changes modes), markitdown, and mammoth model-free. DECISION: drop pandoc entirely, `.docx` becomes `[MammothEngine, MarkitdownEngine]`. Pandoc's sole differentiator turned out to be a live latent bug -- `--track-changes=all` (exactly what convert-md shipped through v0.6.0) rendered tracked changes as HTML-span redline soup (both deleted and inserted text jammed together) injected into the vault; in accept mode it merely equals the light engines, and it left dangling image refs. mammoth (featherweight -- only dep `cobble`) renders clean semantic HTML routed through convert-md's own `source_kind="clean"` path; markitdown stays as the cheap already-a-dep fallback. A load-bearing side-finding (design.md D7): the table divergence was markdownify (markitdown's HTML->md tail) vs html2text, NOT the docx engine -- markitdown wraps mammoth, both consume the same header-less HTML; and routing docx through the generic `convert_html()` was much worse because trafilatura is a web-page boilerplate remover that strips headings and explodes tables on clean fragments (verified trafilatura is still correct on real full pages, so a2web is healthy -- the two paths must never cross). This motivated adding `source_kind: Literal["web_page","clean"]` to `convert_html` (default web_page, a2web untouched): one HTML->markdown render back-end shared by docx and the web path, not a third opinion. Code: `PandocEngine`->`MammothEngine` in engines.py/dispatch.py/__init__; `pypandoc-binary` (a ~100MB+ binary) dropped from `convert-md[documents]`, `mammoth`+`markitdown[docx]` added; `python-docx` added to the dev group as a test-only docx WRITER (convert-md only reads docx). Minor bump 0.6.0 -> 0.7.0 per the shelf's semver discipline for behavior-changing engine swaps. `make check` green (316 passed, 88.69% cov). Open axes left for later (not blockers): OMML math (synthesis-weak), a marked-header table variant, and unifying convert-md on one canonical HTML renderer (its own real-web-corpus BENCH, touches a2web's live path). |
